text: Abstract. This study investigates the photochemical aging of wood-burning aerosol produced under smoldering conditions, utilizing a 104 m³ dual outdoor chamber under natural sunlight across varying relative humidity (RH) and NOx levels. We monitored the light absorption of organic carbon (OC) throughout the photooxidation process using a UV–visible spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere. Our results indicate that high RH leads to rapid color decay, while the presence of NOx slightly extends the lifetime of the aerosol's color, suggesting that NOx facilitates chromophore formation through secondary pathways. Generally, the mass absorption cross-section of OC (integrated from 280 to 600 nm) rose by 11–54% during the morning hours—with the exception of high RH conditions—before gradually declining by 19–68% in the afternoon. This dynamic evolution in light absorption is driven by the competing mechanisms of chromophore formation and sunlight-induced bleaching. To further resolve the relationship between chemical transformation and optical properties, we characterized the particles using multiple spectroscopic techniques. A rapid decrease in fluorescence intensity pointed toward the bleaching of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), while falling levoglucosan concentrations confirmed the steady depletion of primary organic aerosol. Additionally, infrared spectroscopy revealed that the organic particles became less hygroscopic over time. These experimental trends mirror observations of ambient smoke from the 2012 County Line wildfire in Florida. Ultimately, we conclude that biomass-burning OC undergoes a net reduction in light absorption after approximately 8–9 hours of sunlight exposure compared to its initial, fresh state.
